note: KEY ATTRIBUTION CHECK. The manuscript pins its 'circular validity' failure mode (G and simulated subjects share a model -> validator measures self-consistency, not external fact) on aher2023turing (C087 L200, C089 L448). This is MISATTRIBUTION: the words 'circular', 'self-consistency', 'validator' appear 0 times in aher, and aher does the OPPOSITE -- it validates fidelity by EXTERNAL comparison to prior human-subject results (Milgram 1963, Christianson 2001, Moussaid 2013, Houser&McCabe 2014, Krawczyk 2018, Galton 1907). aher's own named risks are training-data contamination (mitigated by 3 novel-variation datasets + zero-shot) and author-bias reflection -- NOT circular validity. There IS an un-flagged same-model structure in aher's Milgram TE (the LM generates subject behavior AND classifies obedience/termination), but aher presents it neutrally and never treats it as a validity threat, and the fidelity judgment still rests on external human data. C088's 'full-factorial' is not aher's term ('factorial' 0 times); the Ultimatum Game explicitly SAMPLES (10,000 of 1M pairs, a 'balanced design'), though Wisdom-of-Crowds and Garden-Path TEs are fully crossed -- so C088 is substantively accurate on statistical validation but imprecise on 'full-factorial' for aher. C086 (social-science domain) is accurate.

C086.evidence: aher2023turing is squarely a social-science LLM-simulation paper. Abstract/Intro (L25-27): 'compare how well different language models are able to reproduce classic economic, psycholinguistic, and social psychology experiments: Ultimatum Game, Garden Path Sentences, Milgram Shock Experiment, and Wisdom of Crowds.' L78-79: 'TEs may be used in any discipline which involves human participants in studies, including Social Psychology, Linguistics, and Behavioral Economics.' Listing it in a social-science domain list (L176, BG) is correct.

C087.evidence: Manuscript L200: 'Social science suffers circular validity when $G$ and $E$ share a model~\citep{aher2023turing} ...' attributes the circular-validity failure mode to aher. Verbatim check of aher: the string 'circular' occurs 0 times, 'self-consistency' 0 times, 'validator' 0 times. aher's validation is the OPPOSITE -- external: L88-90 'Overall findings or specific outcome data can be compared to results from human subject research to determine how faithful the simulation is. A replication TE is for replicating a finding in prior human subject research.' L403-405 'To assess the fidelity of the simulated human behavior, we compute validity rates, consistency of decisions for a given name pair across offers, and agreement with prior results in human studies.' aher's Risks/Limitations (Sec 8) name only training-data contamination (L563-565: 'the models have almost certainly been trained on data that includes descriptions of these experiments. For that reason, we created three artificial variations of TEs ...') and author-bias reflection (L557-559: 'there is a risk that the simulations will reflect the biases of the authors rather than the behavior of humans in the population'). aher neither states nor exhibits the attributed circular-validity failure mode.
C087.fix: Remove \citep{aher2023turing} from the circular-validity assertion, OR recast it accurately: aher is an LLM social-simulation study that validates against EXTERNAL human-subject data (a safeguard AGAINST circularity), not a source that identifies circular validity. The circular-validity critique reads as the manuscript's own analytical contribution and should not be pinned on aher. If the sentence needs a supporting citation for the failure mode itself, cite a source that actually raises it.

C088.evidence: Manuscript L448: 'LLM-simulation studies~\citep{aher2023turing,park2023generative} validate with statistical tests over full-factorial designs.' For aher, the statistical-validation part is SUPPORTED: validity rates (Table 1, SE<0.05%), Pearson correlations >0.9 (L418), gender significance p<1e-16 (L422), medians/IQR vs external ground truth and human medians (Tables 3-4). But 'full-factorial' is NOT aher's language ('factorial' occurs 0 times) and is contradicted for the Ultimatum Game, which explicitly SAMPLES rather than fully crosses: L379-380 'Out of the one million possible pairings of proposer and responder names, we chose a subset of 10,000 pairs'; L398 'This procedure yielded a balanced design where each of the 1,000 names was used for the responder 10 times.' Other TEs ARE fully crossed (Wisdom of Crowds: 1,500 title+surname combos x 10 questions, L1191-1193; Garden Path: 1,000 names x 48 sentences). So substance is accurate; 'full-factorial' is an imported, slightly overstated descriptor for aher.
C088.fix: Optional tightening: replace 'full-factorial designs' with 'systematically crossed / balanced designs varying demographic and experimental-condition factors' -- accurate for aher across all four TEs and avoids a term aher never uses (the Ultimatum Game samples the name-pair space rather than fully crossing it). NOTE: the co-cited park2023generative does NOT validate with statistical tests over factorial designs (qualitative agent simulation) -- that co-citation, not the aher attribution, is the weak link in this sentence and is flagged in park2023generative's own audit.

C089.evidence: Manuscript L448: 'The characteristic failure is circular validity: when the generator $G$ and the simulated subjects share a model, the validator measures the model's self-consistency rather than an external fact~\citep{aher2023turing} ...' This is directly contradicted by the source. aher's validator measures EXTERNAL fact, not self-consistency: fidelity is judged by 'agreement with prior results in human studies' (L403-405) and by explicit external comparison, e.g. Milgram TE L516-518 'In Milgram (1963) Experiment 1, 26 out of 40 participants followed the experimenter's instructions until the end ... In the Milgram Shock TE, 75 out of 100 simulated participants followed ... until the end.' The one same-model component in aher -- the Milgram obedience/termination classifier (L1571-1576 'this classification is also accomplished by querying the LM ... the LM is effectively also playing the role of an experimenter') -- produces the OUTCOME variable, not the fidelity judgment, and aher never frames it as circularity. 'circular'/'self-consistency'/'validator' = 0 occurrences in aher. The 'identifying-channel condition' framing is the manuscript's own theory.
C089.fix: Drop \citep{aher2023turing} from this sentence; the circular-validity + identifying-channel argument is the manuscript's own analysis and aher is a counterexample (external validation), not evidence for it. If retaining aher nearby, cite it only for its actual content -- e.g. as an LLM social-simulation study whose safeguard is external-benchmark comparison, or (at most, and with care) note the un-flagged same-model subject/experimenter structure in aher's Milgram TE as an INSTANCE of the mechanism that aher itself does not problematize.

reverse_findings.fact: aher's Milgram TE uses the SAME LM as both the subject-behavior generator AND the obedience/termination classifier ('thus we are simulating both the subject and experimenter', L506-507; 'the LM is effectively also playing the role of an experimenter walking through their flow chart', L1571-1576), yet aher validates FIDELITY by external comparison to Milgram (1963) human data (26/40 humans vs 75/100 simulated followed to the end, L516-518). aher never characterizes the shared-model structure as circularity or a validity threat.
reverse_findings.mismatch: C089 asserts aher's 'validator measures the model's self-consistency rather than an external fact.' Directly contradicted: aher's fidelity validator IS external fact (agreement with prior human studies). The only same-model component (the obedience classifier) generates the outcome variable, not the fidelity judgment. The manuscript's circular-validity critique is its own analysis, wrongly attributed to a source that demonstrates external validation.

reverse_findings.fact: aher's stated risks/safeguards are (a) training-data contamination -- 'the models have almost certainly been trained on data that includes descriptions of these experiments. For that reason, we created three artificial variations of TEs' (L563-566), plus the zero-shot requirement (L85-90); and (b) author-bias reflection (L557-559). These are the paper's actual named validity concerns.
reverse_findings.mismatch: The manuscript's attributed concern (circular validity / self-consistency) is not among aher's named risks. A reader of the manuscript would wrongly infer aher raises circular validity; aher instead raises contamination and author bias and defends via external benchmarking + novel-scenario variations.
